title: Overview of Housing Discrimination | Mass.gov
content:
# Overview of Housing Discrimination | Mass.gov

Learn about the different forms of housing discrimination and protections under Massachusetts law.

The MCAD is empowered by statute to enforce Massachusetts laws including M.G.L. c. 151B (Anti-discrimination Laws), M.G.L. c. 272, §§92A, 98, 98A (Public Accommodations Law), and M.G.L. c. 111, § 199A (Lead Paint Law). The MCAD is authorized to promulgate procedural regulations (804 CMR 2.00: Discrimination in Housing).

These laws protect you from being treated differently or unfairly based on your membership in a protected class (race, sex, religion, national origin, sexual orientation, familial status, disability, veteran or military service, etc.) by your landlord, management company, or condo association.

## Overview of Illegal Practices

As a property owner, landlord, property manager, mortgage lender, housing authority, voucher administrator, or real estate agent, it is illegal to do the following based on an individual's membership in a protected class:

- Refuse to rent, sell, or negotiate housing or commercial property
- Represent a dwelling as unavailable to push the prospective tenant to another location ("steering")
- Provide different lease, sale terms, or privileges to different people
- Set different conditions or privileges for sale or rental
- Deny access to or membership in a facility or service related to housing
- Impose different rates and terms on a loan
- Refuse to make a mortgage loan
- Discriminate when appraising property
- Make discriminatory inquiries
- Create discriminatory advertisements

## Disability Discrimination

It is illegal to refuse to rent, sell, or deal with a person, or otherwise deny a unit or make it unavailable due to a disability. A landlord may not ask about the nature or severity of a disability, require production of medical records, or assess whether an applicant is capable of living independently.

Public housing must provide reasonable accommodations and modifications. Private housing with 10 or more units must provide modifications at the expense of the owner. Failing to make a reasonable accommodation after a request is illegal.

## Service Animals and Emotional Support Animals

Service animals and emotional support animals are common requests for reasonable accommodation in housing. In Massachusetts, there are no licensing or certification requirements for service animals.

## Families with Children and Familial Status

It is illegal to refuse to sell or rent a dwelling to a family based on the fact that the family includes children. The law prohibits unfair or different treatment of families with children.

## Lead Paint

It is illegal to refuse to sell or rent due to the presence of lead paint. A landlord cannot evict or refuse to rent/renew to families with children under 6 due to lead paint compliance. The Lead Law requires removal or covering of lead paint hazards in homes built before 1978.

## Public Assistance

It is illegal to discriminate against tenants based on receipt of public assistance or refuse to cooperate with public assistance programs, including Section 8, SSDI, RAFT, HomeBASE, Rapid Re-Housing, and Medicare assistance.

## Discriminatory Advertisements

It is illegal to make, print, or publish any notice, statement, or advertisement indicating preference, limitation, or discrimination because of a protected class. Examples: "No Section 8" or "No Kids" signs.
